3D Rendering Project Role: Engineer Tool: Unity URP, HLSL, C#, GitHub Duration: Fall 2025 · 1 semester

Unity URP — View on GitHub Why I wanted to get hands-on with real-time rendering beyond "using shaders" — specifically: how procedural textures, custom shading, and world-space masks can be composed into a readable VFX sequence. This was my final project for CSCI 580: 3D Graphics and Rendering (USC, Fall 2025), where we explored both offline and realtime rendering techniques (rasterization, shading, noise/procedural generation, and pipeline concepts). What A real-time asteroid impact sequence in Unity URP: a procedural asteroid falls onto a terrain, an "impact frame" post-process sells the moment of collision, and an expanding shockwave reveals molten cracks across the ground. Unity URP HLSL C# Procedural Noise World-space Masks How My contributions (Terrain Transformation) I owned the ter

C# Procedural Noise World-space Masks How My contributions (Terrain Transformation) I owned the terrain transformation system — the lava/crack look and the reveal/fade logic that makes the shockwave "paint" the ground as it expands. Procedural lava & cracks shader (HLSL): Built a terrain material that blends from normal ground to molten lava using 3D Worley noise for organic crack boundaries. Corner rounding for Worley junctions: Standard Worley borders can create sharp, unnatural corners at cell junctions. I used the third-nearest distance (F3) to compute a corner metric and widen cracks near junctions, producing rounded lava "veins." Surface micro-detail: Added controllable normal perturbation (Worley + Perlin-style noise) to avoid a flat/plastic feel and improve lighting response. Demo

ation (Worley + Perlin-style noise) to avoid a flat/plastic feel and improve lighting response. Demonstration of the rounding corner effect and normal shading on the lava shader. (left: not rounded; middle: rounded corners; right: added normal details) World-space spherical reveal mask: Implemented a smoothstep -based mask using distance-to-impact-center with radius + feather controls, then used that mask to lerp between the original terrain and lava shader outputs. C# runtime control: Drove shader parameters every frame (center, radius, feather) using MaterialPropertyBlock so multiple objects can share a material while still receiving per-instance reveal behavior. Fade-out variant: Implemented a complementary transparent fade shader using the same world-space mask logic (useful for "clear

ted a complementary transparent fade shader using the same world-space mask logic (useful for "clearing" geometry inside the shockwave region). Controlled revealation of lava and fade-out shaders. Outcomes Team system context This was a 4-person project. While I focused on terrain transformation, the full sequence also includes: Procedural asteroid generation (vertex displacement + heightfield-driven normals) Velocity-reactive asteroid lighting (emission + Fresnel rim "burn") Impact frame post-processing (edge detection + radial blur + polar noise "speed lines") Shockwave bubble shader (depth intersection + Fresnel + heat distortion) What worked well Readable, stylized “lava reveal”: The world-space mask + Worley cracks made the shockwave feel like it physically alters the terrain. More na

ld-space mask + Worley cracks made the shockwave feel like it physically alters the terrain. More natural crack topology: Corner rounding reduced sharp triangular junctions common in basic Worley borders. Reusable control pattern: MaterialPropertyBlock-based driving is engine-friendly and scales to multiple affected surfaces. Limitations & next steps The micro-normal detail was tricky to balance; in the final render we reduced the detail scale to avoid noisy lighting. Next, I’d tune a sharper normal pattern that still reads cleanly in motion. I’d also like to expose a small “art direction” panel (crack width, junction rounding strength, lava pulse speed) for faster iteration. ← Back to Projects

Aegis Sword — a detailed 3D replication of the weapon from Xenoblade Chronicles 2
Aegis Sword Role: CAD Modeler Tool: Onshape CAD Duration: Spring 2021 · 4 weeks

Onshape CAD — View on Onshape CAD Why I wanted to practice translating a stylized game prop into a clean, manufacturable CAD model—especially handling asymmetry, layered details, and tight proportion matching from reference. This was my final project for EAS 143: Introduction to Solid Modeling (University of Rochester, Spring 2021), where the goal was to build accurate parts and assemblies using parametric workflows. What A detailed 3D CAD replication of the Aegis Sword from Xenoblade Chronicles 2 , modeled in Onshape with an emphasis on readable silhouette, precise detailing, and assembly-ready structure. Onshape CAD Parametric Modeling Assembly Surface Detailing Reference Matching How Workflow Reference breakdown: Collected screenshots and orthographic-ish angles, then identified key pro

flow Reference breakdown: Collected screenshots and orthographic-ish angles, then identified key proportions (blade length, guard width, handle thickness) to lock the silhouette early. Modular parts: Split the sword into repeatable subcomponents (blade core, guard, handle, emblem/ornament), so edits wouldn’t cascade across the whole model. Parametric feature tree: Used sketches + extrudes/fillets/patterns with named dimensions to keep the model editable during iteration. Design & detailing Asymmetry handling: Established a consistent datum setup and only mirrored where the design truly repeats; the rest was modeled intentionally off-axis to match the original. Clean edges & readability: Tuned bevels/fillets to avoid “too sharp” transitions while keeping crisp highlights on the main forms.

d bevels/fillets to avoid “too sharp” transitions while keeping crisp highlights on the main forms. Assembly mindset: Modeled as an assembly with part separation in mind (useful for future export, printing, or game-ready retopology workflows). Result The final model reproduces the sword’s layered structure and distinctive asymmetry, and the modular setup made it straightforward to refine proportions and surface details late in the process. ← Back to Projects

Automatic Differentiation Toolbox — a Python package for forward-mode automatic differentiation
Automatic Differentiation (AD) Toolbox Role: Python Developer & QA Lead Tool: Python, PyPI, GitHub Duration: Fall 2022 · 1 semester

Python — View on test.pypi.org Why Derivatives show up everywhere in optimization, ML, and scientific computing, but the common alternatives are frustrating in practice: numerical differentiation can be unstable (step-size tradeoffs), and symbolic differentiation can blow up in expression size. We wanted a small, readable tool that computes exact derivatives at runtime, so users can differentiate “normal Python code” without manually applying the chain rule. What A forward-mode automatic differentiation (AD) Python package built by a 5-person team. It evaluates user-defined functions and returns derivatives by propagating dual numbers through operator overloads and elementary functions. The focus was a clean API, strong correctness guarantees, and maintainability through a test-first workf

focus was a clean API, strong correctness guarantees, and maintainability through a test-first workflow. Python Forward-mode AD Dual Numbers Operator Overloading Unit Testing PyPI How My contributions (Core AD + QA) I implemented key pieces of the forward-mode engine and led QA to keep the package correct as features expanded. Forward-mode engine: Implemented dual number arithmetic and operator overloading to propagate derivatives through composed expressions. Function composition: Ensured derivatives flow correctly through chained operations (add/mul/div/pow, etc.) by applying the chain rule consistently across overloaded operators. Refactoring for clarity: Reduced redundancy and improved readability so teammates could extend the system without breaking core invariants. QA leadership: Des

eadability so teammates could extend the system without breaking core invariants. QA leadership: Designed and executed comprehensive unit tests (core ops + edge cases) to verify numerical correctness and prevent regressions. Debugging & integration: Coordinated module integration, triaged bugs, and documented design decisions to streamline collaboration. Release readiness: Helped ensure stable packaging/behavior for publishing on TestPyPI. ← Back to Projects

Cemented Dreams — Gameplay systems, combat mechanics, and level design.
Cemented Dreams — Gameplay Systems Role: Combat Designer · Gameplay Engineer · Level Designer Tools: Unreal Engine 5, C++, Blueprint

What Cemented Dreams is a third-person action game set inside a parasite-infested building. Players navigate an infected structure where a mechanical Hive grows through the architecture, creating traversal paths and combat spaces. I worked primarily on combat design, gameplay programming, and level design, building core movement and combat mechanics while coordinating closely with artists and producers. Gameplay systems were implemented using a combination of Unreal Engine C++ gameplay code and Blueprint-driven iteration. Main Achievements Designed and implemented core combat movement mechanics including grapple traversal and sliding combat. Built gameplay behaviors using C++ systems exposed to Blueprint for rapid iteration. Designed a level centered around a growing parasite Hive simulati

to Blueprint for rapid iteration. Designed a level centered around a growing parasite Hive simulation integrated into the building structure. Collaborated with artists and producers to align gameplay mechanics with visual direction and milestone goals. Highlights UE5 Gameplay Programming Combat Systems Grapple Mechanics Movement Design Level Design C++ Blueprint How Combat Design + Gameplay Engineering I designed and implemented the player's core movement and combat systems, focusing on mobility and responsiveness in tight indoor environments. Implemented gameplay behaviors such as grapple traversal, sliding movement, and aim-assist targeting. Integrated player input, animation states, and physics interactions through C++ gameplay components and Blueprint interfaces. Exposed key combat pa

physics interactions through C++ gameplay components and Blueprint interfaces. Exposed key combat parameters to designers through Blueprint to enable rapid tuning of movement feel and combat pacing. Worked closely with artists and producers to ensure mechanics supported the intended combat rhythm and player flow. Grapple traversal Aim-assist targeting Level Design — Hive Simulation I designed a level centered around a parasitic Hive structure growing inside a building, creating traversal paths and combat arenas through environmental transformation. The central Hive structure grows into the building while cables from the structure attempt to strangle it. At cable contact points, the Hive generates hexagonal platforms that players can traverse. Entrances into the Hive are torn open by struct

rates hexagonal platforms that players can traverse. Entrances into the Hive are torn open by structural cables, creating layered vertical gameplay spaces. A critical injection point forms around a cable that pierces the Hive wall, granting players the grapple ability. This level structure links narrative worldbuilding with traversal mechanics, turning the parasite's growth into both a visual motif and a gameplay system. CTIN 488 — Game Design Workshop (Fall 2025) This page documents my work across three studio projects in CTIN 488, a playcentric design course focused on rapid prototyping, playtesting, and iteration toward a clear player experience goal. The semester included an Up the River game mod/variation, a focused Mechanics project, and a team Final Project . Use the tabs below to see why we made each project, what we decided to build, and how it evolved through design photo journals. Up the River Mechanics Final Project Up the River: Variation / Game Mod Why Build a variation on a shared base game (Up the River) by changing constraints and mechanics, then iterate through structured playtests while keeping a specific experience goal in focus. What Our variation reworked the original linear track int

eping a specific experience goal in focus. What Our variation reworked the original linear track into a circular race-to-treasure loop with environmental tiles (Wave/Reef), Pirate hazards, and a card system that lets players speed up their ship or hinder others. The intended experience was pressure + strategy as players chase points while avoiding danger. How (weekly iteration) Week 1 — Core loop + first pass balancing (9/21–9/23) Defined the core loop: travel between Port and Treasure Island on a circular track, using dice + event cards to create risk/reward decisions. Iterated quickly on pacing and probability: expanded the board, switched from D4 to D6, increased pirate pressure, and tightened card/rule wording so turns stayed snappy (we focused on pacing/balance tuning + clarity). Week

tened card/rule wording so turns stayed snappy (we focused on pacing/balance tuning + clarity). Week 2 — Playtest-driven clarity + V2 polish (9/24–9/30) Ran a lab playtest and rewrote setup/turn order text for clarity; tuned incentives (e.g., when card draws trigger) to reduce “dead” rolls. Conducted internal playtests to reduce downtime and edge-case confusion (reshuffle rules, clearer card effects, max hand size), then consolidated everything into a cleaner V2 with a faster, smoother flow (we led setup/turn-order + edge-case clarity fixes and helped consolidate the final V2 rules). This project was selected for in-class presentation. Mechanics Project: Focused Mechanic Exploration Why Practice mechanics-first design: choose a core interaction, prototype fast, and use formal feedback to r

tice mechanics-first design: choose a core interaction, prototype fast, and use formal feedback to refine clarity, pacing, and player interpretation. This project started from randomly drawn topics. Our team selected four key words — auction, versus, romance, and solution-finding — and our goal was to turn them into a playable game. We specifically wanted to explore how auctions can function as both a competitive system and a narrative device, letting emotional stakes emerge from mechanical choices (instead of scripted story beats). What Too Bird To Handle is a silent-coordination bidding game themed around birds courting. Players coordinate without speaking, trying to read intention and manage risk through a “give or take” style action loop. Built around an auction-style interaction, the

age risk through a “give or take” style action loop. Built around an auction-style interaction, the experience emphasizes reading intent, managing risk, and negotiating value under social pressure. How (weekly iteration) Week 1 — Prompt → playable prototype + first external tests (Week 9) Used a “yes-and” brainstorm to interpret the prompt, then committed to an auction-inspired, four-player cooperative direction about birds searching for mates. Built a minimum playable prototype: sketched resource and bird cards, established clear win/loss conditions, and iterated internally to smooth the difficulty curve (less swingy, more teamwork). Ran early external playtests and captured the biggest issues: players misread the game as competitive, terminology (“bid/bird”) was confusing, and setup/visi

ues: players misread the game as competitive, terminology (“bid/bird”) was confusing, and setup/visibility needed cleaner structure. Turned playtest feedback into concrete fixes (terminology, setup clarity, table layout), and tightened win/loss wording for faster onboarding. Week 2 — Clarity pass + visual polish + finalization (Week 10) Ran additional external playtests that confirmed stronger engagement and pacing, while still highlighting edge-case questions and moments where the rules needed explicit guidance. Validated changes through internal playtests, then did a final consistency/clarity pass across the rulebook, cards, and board to remove edge cases and improve readability (so the experience matched the intended “silent teamwork” tension). This project was selected for in-class pre

perience matched the intended “silent teamwork” tension). This project was selected for in-class presentation. Final Project: Original Team Game; Role: Producer Why The final project is a free project: define an original experience goal, build an end-to-end prototype, and demonstrate playcentric iteration across multiple weeks (rules, components, balance, and presentation). What Our team built a competitive survival game centered on social hierarchy. Players take on different roles (upper- to lower-class) and must meet role-specific needs inspired by Maslow’s hierarchy while dealing with threats, deception, and negotiation as resources flow from top to bottom each round. How (weekly iteration) Week 1 — Core loop + first playable: We defined the concept, scope, and primary mechanics (roles

n) Week 1 — Core loop + first playable: We defined the concept, scope, and primary mechanics (roles + descending resources), aligned on the intended player experience, and built an early prototype for quick internal/external playtests. We helped lock the core loop and tightened the threat / gun rules based on early feedback. Week 2 — Clarity + agency pass: We expanded and stabilized systems through playtesting, then improved readability and decision-making: “pet” → love, shifted to a single-winner victory condition, increased lower-class agency (starting hand), and refined the rulebook for fewer edge-case questions. We drove rule/wording cleanup and helped implement the agency + win-condition revisions. Week 3 — Balance with simulation support: We ran a focused balance pass supported by 20

n revisions. Week 3 — Balance with simulation support: We ran a focused balance pass supported by 200k-trial simulations, adjusted deck composition, and updated win conditions to reduce “accidental” wins, then validated changes via final playtests. We ran the simulation-backed balance checks and translated results into concrete deck/win-condition updates. Week 4 — Presentation polish: Finalized the slide deck and recorded the final presentation video. We supported deck polish and final deliverable prep. ← Back to Projects

Built a minimum playable prototype: sketched resource and bird cards, established clear win/loss conditions, and iterated internally to smooth the difficulty curve (less swingy, more teamwork). Gyrotris — a sci-fi puzzle game reimagining Tetris with rotating boards, dynamic gravity, and environmental hazards.
Gyrotris Role: Solo Developer Tools: Godot 4.3, GDScript, Piskel

Play on itch.io What Gyrotris is a sci-fi puzzle game that reimagines classic Tetris through rotating boards, dynamic gravity, and environmental hazards. The game is set on a spaceship trapped by a black hole, where the player clears unstable cargo while adapting to shifting conditions. Developed entirely by me for Untitled Game Jam #100, this was my first complete shipped game. I handled the design, programming, and pixel art. Main Achievements Designed a twist on Tetris centered around board rotation and changing gravity. Built the full game solo, including game design, programming, and art. Implemented hazards such as board holes, falling debris, and limited repair tools. Shipped a complete playable prototype within game jam constraints. Highlights Godot GDScript Solo Project Puzzle Des

te playable prototype within game jam constraints. Highlights Godot GDScript Solo Project Puzzle Design Game Jam Pixel Art How Puzzle Design The core design goal was to keep the readability of Tetris while adding new systems that force the player to rethink space, timing, and recovery. Built classic falling-block movement and rotation as the foundation. Added periodic board rotation, which changes how players read and plan the grid. Used dynamic hazards such as holes and debris to disrupt standard settling behavior. Included a time-stop ability and limited repair robots to add strategic recovery options. Solo Development Because this was a fully solo jam project, I focused on building a clear gameplay loop quickly and iterating on the mechanic that made the game distinctive. Implemented th

r gameplay loop quickly and iterating on the mechanic that made the game distinctive. Implemented the full game in Godot 4.3 using GDScript. Created the game's pixel art assets in Piskel. Balanced scope to ship a complete prototype rather than overbuild presentation features. Used the project to explore how established game formulas can be transformed into new experiences. Iteration Gyrotris also gave me an early foundation in iterative design: identifying which mechanic was novel, what players found confusing, and what should be improved in future versions. Evaluated clarity around board rotation feedback and readability. Considered future improvements such as stronger rotation animation and clearer hole behavior. Prime Engine — Engine Systems Implementation (frustum culling, physics/collision, animation blending, debug visualization).
Prime Engine — Engine Systems Implementation Role: Engine Programmer Tool: C++, Prime Engine

What This project involved modifying and extending Prime Engine, a minimal C++ game engine used for gameplay and rendering experiments. I implemented several engine-level systems, including: View frustum culling for rendering optimization Hierarchical BVH culling (static median-split and dynamic Morton-code trees) Collision detection and physics movement (move-and-slide with gravity) Skeletal animation blending (full-body, partial-body, and additive layers) Debug visualization tools for bounding volumes and animation state Working inside the engine required understanding its component system, event system, and scene graph structure in order to integrate new features without breaking the existing architecture. Main Achievements Learned and navigated a large C++ codebase for a minimal custom

ting architecture. Main Achievements Learned and navigated a large C++ codebase for a minimal custom game engine. Implemented view frustum culling for rendering optimization. Built hierarchical BVH culling with static median-split and dynamic Morton-code trees. Implemented a basic physics system with collision and sliding response. Built a skeletal animation blending system supporting full-body, partial-body, and additive blends. Highlights Frustum culling BVH culling Morton codes Physics engine Collision & sliding Animation blending Additive layers Debug visualization C++ How Frustum Culling To reduce unnecessary rendering work, I implemented view frustum culling using bounding boxes. Steps: Compute the world-space bounding box of each mesh instance. Transform the box corners into clip sp

: Compute the world-space bounding box of each mesh instance. Transform the box corners into clip space using the projection–view matrix. Test the box against the six planes of the camera frustum. If all corners lie outside any plane, the object is marked as culled and skipped during rendering. I also added debug rendering for bounding boxes to visually verify the correctness of the culling logic. This significantly reduced the number of objects sent to the renderer when many objects were outside the camera view. Frustum Culling Pipeline Mesh Instance world bounding box Transform corners to clip space Frustum Test 6 plane checks Cull Render outside any plane intersects / inside Bounding box corners are transformed and tested against the camera frustum to skip off-screen objects. Physics an

corners are transformed and tested against the camera frustum to skip off-screen objects. Physics and Collision System I implemented a simple collider system supporting sphere and box colliders attached to game objects. Each frame: Colliders update their world-space position from the owning object's transform. When an object attempts to move, the engine checks for collisions at the candidate position. If collisions occur, the movement vector is projected onto the collision plane, removing the component that pushes into the surface. The remaining movement causes the object to slide naturally along obstacles. Gravity For objects affected by gravity: Vertical velocity is updated each frame using gravitational acceleration. The vertical component of movement is applied to the object. If ground

g gravitational acceleration. The vertical component of movement is applied to the object. If ground support is detected, vertical velocity resets to prevent continuous downward acceleration. This produces natural falling behavior while preventing objects from penetrating the ground. Physics: Move-and-Slide with Gravity Input Move desired displacement Apply Gravity update vertical velocity Collision Query check candidate position No Hit → Move Hit Surface remove normal component Slide move along tangent gravity Candidate movement is tested for collisions; if blocked, the engine cancels motion into the surface and keeps the tangential part for sliding. Animation Blending I extended the soldier's animation state machine with several blending modes, chosen at spawn via an npcType value from L

r's animation state machine with several blending modes, chosen at spawn via an npcType value from Lua. The skeleton is split into an upper body (joints 2–37) and a lower body (joints 38–47), so clips can drive different parts at once. Full-body blend — two clips mixed by a factor (e.g. idle + run, weighted 0.5 each). Partial blend — a clip overrides only a joint range via PARTIAL_BODY_ANIMATION (e.g. upper-body shoot over a full-body idle). Additive blend — a clip layered on top of the existing pose instead of replacing it. For additive motion I added an ADDITIVE slot flag. The additive pass takes the clip's delta from its reference pose (frame 0) as a rotation-only quaternion and pre-multiplies it onto the joint's local transform — so a base motion stays intact while extra movement is la

plies it onto the joint's local transform — so a base motion stays intact while extra movement is layered on (e.g. lower-body run + upper-body shoot, running and aiming at once). Animation Blending Modes Full-body blend STAND + RUN 0.5 / 0.5 across all joints Partial blend Shoot (2–37) STAND (lower) upper-body override replaces joints 2–37 Additive blend STAND + Shoot RUN (38–47) layered on top delta pose added to upper body Hierarchical Culling — Static & Dynamic BVH I extended frustum culling into a Bounding Volume Hierarchy managed by a central CullingManager , so large groups of objects can be rejected with a few AABB-vs-frustum tests instead of one test per mesh. Each instance is bucketed as static or dynamic. Static BVH — built once, recursively median-split along the widest centroid

ed as static or dynamic. Static BVH — built once, recursively median-split along the widest centroid axis, with leaves holding ≤ 8 instances. Traversal prunes whole subtrees whose bounds miss the frustum. Dynamic BVH — rebuilt every frame for moving objects (to stress-test it I spawned many small meshes given simple random movement). Centroids are normalized and encoded as 30-bit Morton codes (Z-order), sorted, then split at the midpoint — a linear, cache-friendly construction. The static tree gives better quality for buildings and terrain, while the Morton-code rebuild is cheap enough to run per frame for many moving units — Z-order keeps spatially close objects adjacent in memory, so the build and traversal access nodes nearly linearly, staying cache-friendly. Both are roughly O(n log n)

uild and traversal access nodes nearly linearly, staying cache-friendly. Both are roughly O(n log n) , dominated by the sort. Static vs. Dynamic BVH Construction Static BVH — median split root AABB split on widest axis · leaves ≤ 8 instances built once for static geometry Dynamic BVH — Morton (Z-order) Morton code → sort → midpoint split rebuilt every frame for moving objects Static BVH — rainbow boxes from root (red) to leaf (purple). Dynamic BVH — white boxes over randomly moving meshes, leaves shown as dots. ← Back to Projects

Instead of piloting the protagonist moment-to-moment, players design a route and then watch their plan unfold, for better or worse, as environmental objects react in surprising ways. The appeal—and the difficulty—comes from predicting emergent interactions while still feeling empowered by a plan that was yours. My responsibilities covered concept ideation, systems design, engineering, and integration of artist-created assets in Godot. The core loop began with a lightweight pathing tool, but the heart of the experience emerged from environmental 'verbs': train tracks, whirlpools, spring pads, trapd

t of the experience emerged from environmental 'verbs': train tracks, whirlpools, spring pads, trapdoors, and chain reactions that could delight or derail. Balancing readability with surprise required strict rules and effective feedback animation that help the player learn the world's grammar. Difficulty tuning was driven by data rather than guesswork; I exposed parameters for moving speeds, impulse strength, and trigger sequencing, allowing tight loops of test–tweak–test within the jam's limited timeframe. The art pass focused on clarity and charm: silhouettes that read at a glance, restrained palettes, and motion that communicates function. We achieved a polished, playable demo on schedule and ranked in the top 20% overall. Post-jam, I'd extend the system with better text and visual expl

and ranked in the top 20% overall. Post-jam, I'd extend the system with better text and visual explanation so players can gradually learn the mechanics, add optional objectives for mastery, and implement robust replays to encourage sharing. Most importantly, the project validated a personal design interest: agency through foresight. When players laugh at a plan that almost worked—and instantly want to iterate—you've got a loop worth growing. chunks.page_title: AI chat agent project (LLM, RAG, agents)
chunks.section_title: AI chat agent project (LLM, RAG, agents)
chunks.text: LLM and AI agent engineering: YC designed and built the AI chat agent on this website end-to-end. It is a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) system: site content is chunked and embedded into a static vector index, sentence embeddings run in the visitor's browser (MiniLM via transformers.js / ONNX), retrieval is client-side vector search, and answer generation uses an LLM served through a Cloudflare Worker. The system includes role-conditioned prompting, prompt-injection and off-topic defenses, per-turn logging for evaluation, and a tested Python ingestion pipeline. It demonstrates practical experience with LLM applications, embeddings, semantic search, and agent-style system design.

Machine learning background: ML and applied Bayesian analysis coursework at Harvard Medical School (biomedical informatics master's), the Automatic Differentiation toolbox project (Python developer & QA lead), and peer-reviewed publications applying machine learning to medical imaging (ps-KDE, semantic segmentation of chest X-ray images, PLOS ONE; NeuroImage neurocognitive research).

Unity URP — 在 GitHub 上查看 为什么做 我想真正上手实时渲染，而不只是停留在“会用着色器”的层面：具体来说，就是如何把程序化纹理、自定义着色与世界空间遮罩组合成一段清晰可读的视觉特效序列。 这是我在 CSCI 580：3D 图形与渲染课程（南加州大学，2025 秋季）的期末项目，课程中我们探索了离线与实时的渲染技术（光栅化、着色、噪声/程序化生成，以及管线相关概念）。 做了什么 一段在 Unity URP 中实现的实时小行星撞击序列：一颗程序化生成的小行星坠向地形，一个“撞击帧”后处理强化了碰撞瞬间的冲击感，随后不断扩张的冲击波在地面上揭示出熔融的裂缝。 Unity URP HLSL C# Procedural Noise World-space Masks 如何实现 我的贡献（地形变换） 我负责整个地形变换系统：熔岩/裂缝的外观，以及让冲击波在扩张时“绘制”地面的显现/淡出逻辑。 程序化熔岩与裂缝着色器（HLSL）：构建了一种地形材质，利用 3D Worley 噪声生成自然的裂缝边界，从普通地面平滑过渡到熔融熔岩。 Worley 交界处的圆角处理：标准的 Worley 边界会在单元交界处产生尖锐、不自然的拐角。我使用第三近距离（F3）计算拐角度量，并在交界附近加宽裂缝，从而形成圆润的熔岩“脉络”。 表面微观细节：加入可控的法线扰动（Worley + Perlin 风格噪声），避免出现扁平/塑料感，并改善光照响应。 熔岩着色器上圆角效果与法线着色的演示。 （左：未圆角；中：圆角；右：加入法线细节） 世界空间球形显现遮罩：基于 smoothstep 实现了一个遮罩，使用到撞击中心的距离并配以半径 + 羽化控制，再用该遮罩在原始地形与熔岩着色器输出之间进行插值。 C# 运行时控制：使用 MaterialPropertyBlock 每帧驱动着色器参数（中心、半

遮罩，使用到撞击中心的距离并配以半径 + 羽化控制，再用该遮罩在原始地形与熔岩着色器输出之间进行插值。 C# 运行时控制：使用 MaterialPropertyBlock 每帧驱动着色器参数（中心、半径、羽化），使多个对象可以共享同一材质，同时仍获得逐实例的显现表现。 淡出变体：使用相同的世界空间遮罩逻辑实现了一个互补的透明淡出着色器（可用于“清除”冲击波范围内的几何体）。 熔岩显现与淡出着色器的受控演示。 成果 团队系统背景 这是一个 4 人项目。在我专注于地形变换的同时，整个序列还包括： 程序化小行星生成（顶点位移 + 高度场驱动的法线） 随速度响应的小行星光照（自发光 + 菲涅尔边缘“灼烧”） 撞击帧后处理（边缘检测 + 径向模糊 + 极坐标噪声“速度线”） 冲击波气泡着色器（深度相交 + 菲涅尔 + 热浪扭曲） 做得好的地方 清晰而风格化的“熔岩显现”：世界空间遮罩 + Worley 裂缝让冲击波仿佛真的在物理改变地形。 更自然的裂缝拓扑：圆角处理减少了基础 Worley 边界中常见的尖锐三角形交界。 可复用的控制模式：基于 MaterialPropertyBlock 的驱动方式对引擎友好，并能扩展到多个受影响的表面。 局限与后续计划 微观法线细节的平衡比较棘手；在最终渲染中，我们降低了细节强度以避免光照出现噪点。接下来，我会调出一种更锐利、在运动中仍然清晰可读的法线图案。 我还希望开放一个小巧的“美术调控”面板（裂缝宽度、交界圆角强度、熔岩脉动速度），以加快迭代。 ← 返回项目

Aegis Sword 职责： CAD 建模师 工具： Onshape CAD 时长： 2021 年春季 · 4 周

Onshape CAD — 在 Onshape CAD 中查看 为什么做 我想练习把一个风格化的游戏道具转化为干净、可制造的 CAD 模型，尤其是处理不对称结构、层叠细节，以及严格对照参考图匹配比例。 这是我在 EAS 143：实体建模导论（罗切斯特大学，2021 年春季）的期末项目，目标是运用参数化工作流构建精确的零件与装配体。 做了什么 这是对 Xenoblade Chronicles 2 中 Aegis Sword 的一次精细三维 CAD 复刻，在 Onshape 中建模，注重清晰的剪影轮廓、精准的细节刻画，以及便于装配的结构。 Onshape CAD Parametric Modeling Assembly Surface Detailing Reference Matching 如何实现 工作流程 拆解参考：收集截图与接近正交视角的角度图，找出关键比例（剑身长度、护手宽度、剑柄粗细），从而尽早确定剪影轮廓。 模块化零件：将剑拆分为可复用的子部件（剑身主体、护手、剑柄、纹章/装饰件），这样一处修改不会牵动整个模型。 参数化特征树：使用草图配合拉伸、圆角、阵列等特征，并为尺寸命名，使模型在迭代过程中始终可编辑。 设计与细节 处理不对称：建立一套统一的基准，仅在设计确实重复之处使用镜像；其余部分刻意偏离轴线建模，以贴合原作。 干净的边缘与可读性：调整倒角与圆角，避免过于生硬的过渡，同时在主要形体上保留清晰的高光。 装配思维：以装配体的方式建模，并考虑零件的分件（便于日后导出、打印，或面向游戏的重拓扑流程）。 成果 最终模型还原了这把剑的层叠结构与鲜明的不对称感，而模块化的搭建方式让我在流程后期也能轻松打磨比例与表面细节。 ← 返回项目

Automatic Differentiation (AD) Toolbox 职责： Python 开发者 & QA 负责人 工具： Python、PyPI、GitHub 周期： 2022 年秋 · 一学期

Python — 在 test.pypi.org 查看 为什么做 导数在优化、机器学习和科学计算中无处不在，但常见的几种做法在实践中都很别扭：数值微分不够稳定（受步长权衡的影响），符号微分则可能让表达式规模急剧膨胀。 我们想要一个小巧、易读的工具，能在运行时计算精确的导数，让用户可以对“普通的 Python 代码”求导，而无需手动套用链式法则。 做了什么 一个由 5 人团队开发的前向模式自动微分（AD）Python 包。它对用户自定义的函数求值，并通过在运算符重载和基本函数中传播对偶数来返回导数。 开发重点在于简洁的 API、可靠的正确性保证，以及通过测试先行的工作流带来的可维护性。 Python Forward-mode AD Dual Numbers Operator Overloading Unit Testing PyPI 如何实现 我的贡献（核心 AD + QA） 我实现了前向模式引擎的关键部分，并主导 QA 工作，在功能不断扩展的同时确保包的正确性。 前向模式引擎：实现了对偶数运算和运算符重载，以在复合表达式中传播导数。 函数复合：通过在各重载运算符中一致地应用链式法则，确保导数在链式运算（加/乘/除/幂等）中正确传递。 重构以提升清晰度：减少冗余、改善可读性，让队友能在不破坏核心不变量的前提下扩展系统。 QA 主导：设计并执行了全面的单元测试（核心运算 + 边界情况），以验证数值正确性并防止回归。 调试与集成：协调模块集成、分类处理缺陷，并记录设计决策，以让协作更顺畅。 发布准备：协助确保打包与行为的稳定，以便在 TestPyPI 上发布。 ← 返回项目

做了什么 Cemented Dreams 是一款第三人称动作游戏，故事发生在一座被寄生体侵蚀的建筑内部。玩家在受感染的结构中穿行，一座机械 Hive 在建筑中不断生长，形成位移路径与战斗空间。 我主要负责战斗设计、玩法编程与关卡设计，构建核心的移动与战斗机制，同时与美术和制作人密切协作。 玩法系统通过 Unreal Engine 的 C++ 玩法代码与 Blueprint 驱动的迭代相结合来实现。 主要成果 设计并实现了核心的战斗移动机制，包括钩爪位移与滑行战斗。 使用暴露给 Blueprint 的 C++ 系统构建玩法行为，以便快速迭代。 设计了一个以不断生长的寄生 Hive 模拟为核心、并融入建筑结构的关卡。 与美术和制作人协作，使玩法机制与视觉方向及里程碑目标保持一致。 亮点 UE5 Gameplay Programming Combat Systems Grapple Mechanics Movement Design Level Design C++ Blueprint 如何实现 战斗设计 + 玩法工程 我设计并实现了玩家的核心移动与战斗系统，专注于狭窄室内环境中的机动性与操作响应。 实现了钩爪位移、滑行移动与瞄准辅助等玩法行为。 通过 C++ 玩法组件与 Blueprint 接口整合了玩家输入、动画状态与物理交互。 通过 Blueprint 将关键战斗参数开放给设计师，以便快速调校移动手感与战斗节奏。 与美术和制作人密切协作，确保机制契合预期的战斗节奏与玩家流程。 钩爪位移 瞄准辅助 关卡设计：Hive 模拟 我设计了一个以寄生 Hive 结构在建筑内部生长为核心的关卡，通过环境的变形创造出位移路径与战斗场地。 中央的 Hive 结构向建筑内部生长，而建筑的缆索则试图将其绞杀。 在缆索的接触点，Hive 会生成供玩家穿行的六边形平台。 进入 Hive 的入口被结

Codebreaker — 玩法系统 职责： 玩法工程师 · 系统设计师 工具： Unity 6、C#

做了什么 Codebreaker 是一款基于系统设计、使用 Unity 开发的动作游戏，聚焦于战斗互动，以及玩家在结构化场景中的决策。 我是该项目的主要玩法工程师，负责实现核心玩法架构与战斗系统，并与制作和音频团队协作，将各项功能整合进里程碑版本。 项目的目标是设计一套玩法系统，以支持清晰的玩家反馈、灵敏的操作手感，以及稳定的战斗节奏。 主要成果 实现了核心玩法系统，包括战斗机制、玩家互动与场景逻辑。 设计了玩法架构，支持模块化系统与清晰的事件驱动互动。 将动画、音频触发与玩家反馈整合为连贯的玩法循环。 与制作和音频团队协作，交付可试玩的里程碑版本。 亮点 Unity Gameplay Programming Combat Systems Game Architecture Player Interaction C# 如何实现 玩法系统架构 我设计了驱动玩家互动与战斗行为的核心玩法系统。这些系统负责管理玩家动作、场景响应与玩法反馈。 实现了玩家战斗动作与互动机制的玩法逻辑。 设计了模块化的玩法脚本，以支持灵活的功能开发与迭代。 整合了玩家输入、动画与玩法反馈，以确保操作灵敏。 对玩法系统做了结构化设计，使机制可以在不破坏现有系统的前提下扩展。 游戏手感与玩家反馈 本项目的一大重点，是确保玩家的每个动作都能获得清晰而即时的反馈。 整合了动画事件与音频触发，以强化玩法动作的表现力。 实现了反馈系统，用于传达战斗结果与玩家互动。 与音频和制作团队协作，确保玩法事件触发正确的视听反馈。 关卡互动设计 玩法空间的设计旨在支撑战斗节奏，并创造互动机会。 设计了鼓励玩家移动与战术决策的关卡互动。 实现了与玩法事件相连的场景互动系统。 确保玩法系统能够支持设计师对关卡流程与玩家挑战进行迭代。 ← 返回项目

chunks.section_title: CTIN 488：游戏设计工作坊（2025 秋季）
chunks.text: CTIN 488：游戏设计工作坊（2025 秋季） 本页记录了我在 CTIN 488 中完成的三个工作室项目。这是一门以玩法为核心的设计课程，聚焦于快速原型、玩法测试，以及朝着清晰的玩家体验目标不断迭代。本学期包含一个 Up the River 游戏 mod／变体、一个专注的 Mechanics 项目，以及一个团队 Final Project 。 点击下方标签页，即可了解我们为什么要做每个项目、决定构建什么，以及它如何通过设计照片日志逐步演进。 Up the River Mechanics Final Project Up the River：变体／游戏 Mod 为什么 在一个共享的基础游戏（Up the River）之上，通过改变约束与机制来打造一个变体，然后在始终聚焦于特定体验目标的前提下，通过结构化的玩法测试不断迭代。 做了什么 我们的变体把原本的线性轨道改造成一个环形的夺宝竞赛回路，加入了环境地块（Wave／Reef）、海盗危险，以及一套可以让玩家加速自己船只或阻碍他人的卡牌系统。预期的体验是压力与策略并存：玩家在追逐分数的同时，还要不断躲避危险。 如何实现（每周迭代） 第 1 周：核心循环 + 首轮平衡调整（9/21–9/23） 确定了核心循环：在环形轨道上于 Port 和 Treasure Island 之间移动，用骰子 + 事件卡制造风险／收益的抉择。 在节奏与概率上快速迭代：扩大了棋盘，把 D4 换成 D6，加大海盗压力，并收紧卡牌／规则的措辞，让每个回合都保持利落（我们专注于节奏／平衡的调校与清晰度）。 第 2 周：以玩法测试驱动清晰度 + V2 打磨（9/24–9/30） 进行了一次实验室玩法测试，并为清晰起见重写了布置／回合顺序的说明；调整了激励机制（例如抽牌的触发时机），以减少“无效”的掷骰。 开展了内部玩法测试，以减少等待时间与边缘情况带来的困惑（

chunks.section_title: CTIN 488：游戏设计工作坊（2025 秋季）
chunks.text: 4–9/30） 进行了一次实验室玩法测试，并为清晰起见重写了布置／回合顺序的说明；调整了激励机制（例如抽牌的触发时机），以减少“无效”的掷骰。 开展了内部玩法测试，以减少等待时间与边缘情况带来的困惑（重洗规则、更清晰的卡牌效果、最大手牌数），随后把一切整合成更简洁的 V2，流程更快也更顺畅（我们主导了布置／回合顺序与边缘情况的清晰化修正，并协助整合了最终的 V2 规则）。 该项目入选了课堂展示。 Mechanics 项目：聚焦机制的探索 为什么 练习机制优先的设计：选定一个核心交互，快速制作原型，并借助正式反馈来打磨清晰度、节奏和玩家的理解。 这个项目从随机抽取的主题开始。我们团队选定了四个关键词：拍卖、对抗、浪漫与寻找解法，目标是把它们变成一款可玩的游戏。 我们尤其想探索拍卖如何既能作为一套竞争系统，又能作为一种叙事手段，让情感张力从机制选择中自然浮现，而不是依赖预设的剧情节点。 做了什么 Too Bird To Handle 是一款以鸟类求偶为主题的无声协作竞价游戏。玩家在不开口的情况下相互配合，通过“取或舍”式的行动循环来揣摩意图、管理风险。 整个体验围绕拍卖式的交互构建，强调在社交压力下解读意图、管理风险并权衡价值。 如何实现（每周迭代） 第 1 周：从命题到可玩原型 + 首次外部测试（第 9 周） 用“yes-and”式的头脑风暴来解读命题，随后确定了一个受拍卖启发、以鸟类寻偶为主题的四人合作方向。 制作了一个最小可玩原型：勾勒了资源卡与鸟类卡，确立了清晰的胜负条件，并在内部迭代以平滑难度曲线（波动更小、更讲究团队协作）。 进行了早期的外部玩法测试，并记录下最主要的问题：玩家误以为这是竞争型游戏，术语（“bid/bird”）容易混淆，布置与可见性也需要更清晰的结构。 把玩法测试的反馈转化为具体修改（术语、布置的清晰度、桌面布局），并收紧了胜负条件的措辞，让上手更快

chunks.section_title: CTIN 488：游戏设计工作坊（2025 秋季）
chunks.text: 题：玩家误以为这是竞争型游戏，术语（“bid/bird”）容易混淆，布置与可见性也需要更清晰的结构。 把玩法测试的反馈转化为具体修改（术语、布置的清晰度、桌面布局），并收紧了胜负条件的措辞，让上手更快。 第 2 周：清晰度梳理 + 视觉打磨 + 定稿（第 10 周） 又进行了几次外部玩法测试，确认了更强的投入感与更好的节奏，同时也暴露出一些边缘情况的疑问，以及规则需要明确指引的地方。 通过内部玩法测试验证了这些改动，然后对规则书、卡牌与棋盘做了最后一轮一致性／清晰度梳理，以消除边缘情况、提升可读性（让体验契合预期中“无声协作”的张力）。 该项目入选了课堂展示。 Final Project：原创团队游戏；职责：制作人 为什么 期末项目是一个自由命题：确定一个原创的体验目标，构建一个端到端的原型，并在数周时间里展现以玩法为核心的迭代（规则、组件、平衡与展示）。 做了什么 我们团队打造了一款以社会阶层为核心的竞争型生存游戏。玩家扮演不同的角色（从上层到下层），必须满足受马斯洛需求层次启发、因角色而异的需求，同时应对威胁、欺骗与谈判，而资源在每一回合都自上而下流动。 如何实现（每周迭代） 第 1 周：核心循环 + 首个可玩版本： 我们确定了概念、范围与主要机制（角色 + 逐级下降的资源），就预期的玩家体验达成一致， 并搭建了早期原型以进行快速的内部／外部玩法测试。 我们帮助锁定了核心循环，并根据早期反馈收紧了威胁／枪支规则。 第 2 周：清晰度 + 能动性梳理： 我们通过玩法测试扩展并稳定了各个系统，随后提升了可读性与决策感： “pet” → love，改为单一胜者的胜利条件，增强了下层阶级的能动性（初始手牌）， 并精修规则书以减少边缘情况的疑问。 我们推动了规则／措辞的清理，并协助实现了能动性与胜利条件的修订。 第 3 周：借助模拟的平衡调整： 我们在 20 万次试验的模拟支持下

chunks.section_title: CTIN 488：游戏设计工作坊（2025 秋季）
chunks.text: 层阶级的能动性（初始手牌）， 并精修规则书以减少边缘情况的疑问。 我们推动了规则／措辞的清理，并协助实现了能动性与胜利条件的修订。 第 3 周：借助模拟的平衡调整： 我们在 20 万次试验的模拟支持下进行了一轮专注的平衡调整，调整了牌库构成， 并更新了胜利条件以减少“意外”取胜，随后通过最终玩法测试验证了这些改动。 我们运行了以模拟为依据的平衡检查，并把结果转化为具体的牌库／胜利条件更新。 第 4 周：展示打磨： 完成了幻灯片定稿并录制了最终的展示视频。 我们协助了幻灯片打磨与最终交付物的准备。 ← 返回项目

chunks.section_title: Up the River：变体／游戏 Mod
chunks.text: Up the River：变体／游戏 Mod 为什么 在一个共享的基础游戏（Up the River）之上，通过改变约束与机制来打造一个变体，然后在始终聚焦于特定体验目标的前提下，通过结构化的玩法测试不断迭代。 做了什么 我们的变体把原本的线性轨道改造成一个环形的夺宝竞赛回路，加入了环境地块（Wave／Reef）、海盗危险，以及一套可以让玩家加速自己船只或阻碍他人的卡牌系统。预期的体验是压力与策略并存：玩家在追逐分数的同时，还要不断躲避危险。 如何实现（每周迭代） 第 1 周：核心循环 + 首轮平衡调整（9/21–9/23） 确定了核心循环：在环形轨道上于 Port 和 Treasure Island 之间移动，用骰子 + 事件卡制造风险／收益的抉择。 在节奏与概率上快速迭代：扩大了棋盘，把 D4 换成 D6，加大海盗压力，并收紧卡牌／规则的措辞，让每个回合都保持利落（我们专注于节奏／平衡的调校与清晰度）。 第 2 周：以玩法测试驱动清晰度 + V2 打磨（9/24–9/30） 进行了一次实验室玩法测试，并为清晰起见重写了布置／回合顺序的说明；调整了激励机制（例如抽牌的触发时机），以减少“无效”的掷骰。 开展了内部玩法测试，以减少等待时间与边缘情况带来的困惑（重洗规则、更清晰的卡牌效果、最大手牌数），随后把一切整合成更简洁的 V2，流程更快也更顺畅（我们主导了布置／回合顺序与边缘情况的清晰化修正，并协助整合了最终的 V2 规则）。 该项目入选了课堂展示。

chunks.section_title: Mechanics 项目：聚焦机制的探索
chunks.text: Mechanics 项目：聚焦机制的探索 为什么 练习机制优先的设计：选定一个核心交互，快速制作原型，并借助正式反馈来打磨清晰度、节奏和玩家的理解。 这个项目从随机抽取的主题开始。我们团队选定了四个关键词：拍卖、对抗、浪漫与寻找解法，目标是把它们变成一款可玩的游戏。 我们尤其想探索拍卖如何既能作为一套竞争系统，又能作为一种叙事手段，让情感张力从机制选择中自然浮现，而不是依赖预设的剧情节点。 做了什么 Too Bird To Handle 是一款以鸟类求偶为主题的无声协作竞价游戏。玩家在不开口的情况下相互配合，通过“取或舍”式的行动循环来揣摩意图、管理风险。 整个体验围绕拍卖式的交互构建，强调在社交压力下解读意图、管理风险并权衡价值。 如何实现（每周迭代） 第 1 周：从命题到可玩原型 + 首次外部测试（第 9 周） 用“yes-and”式的头脑风暴来解读命题，随后确定了一个受拍卖启发、以鸟类寻偶为主题的四人合作方向。 制作了一个最小可玩原型：勾勒了资源卡与鸟类卡，确立了清晰的胜负条件，并在内部迭代以平滑难度曲线（波动更小、更讲究团队协作）。 进行了早期的外部玩法测试，并记录下最主要的问题：玩家误以为这是竞争型游戏，术语（“bid/bird”）容易混淆，布置与可见性也需要更清晰的结构。 把玩法测试的反馈转化为具体修改（术语、布置的清晰度、桌面布局），并收紧了胜负条件的措辞，让上手更快。 第 2 周：清晰度梳理 + 视觉打磨 + 定稿（第 10 周） 又进行了几次外部玩法测试，确认了更强的投入感与更好的节奏，同时也暴露出一些边缘情况的疑问，以及规则需要明确指引的地方。 通过内部玩法测试验证了这些改动，然后对规则书、卡牌与棋盘做了最后一轮一致性／清晰度梳理，以消除边缘情况、提升可读性（让体验契合预期中“无声协作”的张力）。 该项目入选了课堂展示。

chunks.section_title: Final Project：原创团队游戏；职责：制作人
chunks.text: Final Project：原创团队游戏；职责：制作人 为什么 期末项目是一个自由命题：确定一个原创的体验目标，构建一个端到端的原型，并在数周时间里展现以玩法为核心的迭代（规则、组件、平衡与展示）。 做了什么 我们团队打造了一款以社会阶层为核心的竞争型生存游戏。玩家扮演不同的角色（从上层到下层），必须满足受马斯洛需求层次启发、因角色而异的需求，同时应对威胁、欺骗与谈判，而资源在每一回合都自上而下流动。 如何实现（每周迭代） 第 1 周：核心循环 + 首个可玩版本： 我们确定了概念、范围与主要机制（角色 + 逐级下降的资源），就预期的玩家体验达成一致， 并搭建了早期原型以进行快速的内部／外部玩法测试。 我们帮助锁定了核心循环，并根据早期反馈收紧了威胁／枪支规则。 第 2 周：清晰度 + 能动性梳理： 我们通过玩法测试扩展并稳定了各个系统，随后提升了可读性与决策感： “pet” → love，改为单一胜者的胜利条件，增强了下层阶级的能动性（初始手牌）， 并精修规则书以减少边缘情况的疑问。 我们推动了规则／措辞的清理，并协助实现了能动性与胜利条件的修订。 第 3 周：借助模拟的平衡调整： 我们在 20 万次试验的模拟支持下进行了一轮专注的平衡调整，调整了牌库构成， 并更新了胜利条件以减少“意外”取胜，随后通过最终玩法测试验证了这些改动。 我们运行了以模拟为依据的平衡检查，并把结果转化为具体的牌库／胜利条件更新。 第 4 周：展示打磨： 完成了幻灯片定稿并录制了最终的展示视频。 我们协助了幻灯片打磨与最终交付物的准备。
Gyrotris 职责： 独立开发者 工具： Godot 4.3、GDScript、Piskel

在 itch.io 上试玩 做了什么 Gyrotris 是一款科幻解谜游戏，借助棋盘旋转、动态重力和环境危险，重新演绎了经典的 Tetris。游戏设定在一艘被黑洞困住的飞船上，玩家一边清理不稳定的货物，一边适应不断变化的环境。 这款游戏由我独自为 Untitled Game Jam #100 开发，是我第一款完整发布的作品。设计、编程和像素美术均由我一人完成。 主要成果 设计了对 Tetris 的创新玩法，核心围绕棋盘旋转与重力变化。 独自完成了整款游戏，涵盖游戏设计、编程与美术。 实现了多种危险机制，例如棋盘空洞、坠落的碎片以及数量有限的维修工具。 在 game jam 的限制条件下，发布了一个完整可玩的原型。 亮点 Godot GDScript Solo Project Puzzle Design Game Jam Pixel Art 如何实现 解谜设计 核心设计目标是保留 Tetris 的易读性，同时加入新的系统，迫使玩家重新思考空间、时机与补救。 以经典的方块下落移动与旋转作为基础。 加入了周期性的棋盘旋转，改变了玩家阅读和规划网格的方式。 利用空洞、碎片等动态危险，打破常规的方块堆叠方式。 加入了时间停止能力和数量有限的维修机器人，增加了战略性的补救选择。 独立开发 由于这是一个完全独立的 jam 项目，我专注于快速搭建清晰的玩法循环，并不断打磨让游戏与众不同的机制。 使用 GDScript 在 Godot 4.3 中实现了整款游戏。 在 Piskel 中制作了游戏的像素美术资源。 权衡了开发范围，选择发布一个完整的原型，而非过度堆砌表现层的功能。 借这个项目探索如何将成熟的游戏套路转化为全新的体验。 迭代 Gyrotris 也让我打下了迭代设计的早期基础：判断哪种机制真正新颖、玩家在哪里感到困惑，以及未来版本应改进之处。 评估了棋盘旋转反馈的清晰度与易读性。 考
将成熟的游戏套路转化为全新的体验。 迭代 Gyrotris 也让我打下了迭代设计的早期基础：判断哪种机制真正新颖、玩家在哪里感到困惑，以及未来版本应改进之处。 评估了棋盘旋转反馈的清晰度与易读性。 考虑了未来的改进，例如更强的旋转动画和更清晰的空洞表现。 借助玩法测试的反馈，思考多少混乱程度有助于游戏，又在多大程度上损害易读性。 ← 返回项目

Nothing Can Go Wrong 职责：主程序 工具：Godot 4、GDScript

在 itch.io 上试玩 做了什么 Nothing Can Go Wrong 是一款在游戏 jam 期间制作的快节奏游戏，目标是在极短的开发周期内设计并交付一个完整可玩的体验。 我担任主程序，负责实现核心玩法系统、玩家操控与游戏逻辑，并整合队友制作的素材。 项目专注于快速迭代与实验，同时在整个 jam 期间始终保持稳定、可玩的构建版本。 主要成果 实现了核心玩法系统与玩家操控逻辑。 使用 Godot 整合了游戏机制、UI 反馈与输入系统。 在严格的时间限制下快速迭代玩法机制。 在游戏 jam 截止前交付了打磨完善的可玩版本。 亮点 Godot GDScript Gameplay Programming Rapid Prototyping Game Jam 如何实现 快速玩法实现 由于 jam 时间紧张，主要的工程目标是构建简单、模块化且易于迭代的玩法系统。 使用 Godot 与 GDScript 实现了核心玩法机制与玩家操控逻辑。 构建了将输入处理、游戏状态与玩家反馈串联起来的系统。 合理组织脚本结构，使新机制能在玩法测试中被快速添加或调整。 快速迭代与调试 游戏 jam 需要不断迭代与快速解决问题。开发过程中包含频繁的玩法测试与调整，以确保游戏始终有趣且稳定。 根据快速玩法测试的反馈迭代玩法机制。 调试玩法问题并优化脚本，以保持流畅的性能。 在快速添加新功能的同时确保游戏保持稳定。 ← 返回项目

chunks.section_title: Prime Engine — 引擎系统实现
chunks.text: Prime Engine — 引擎系统实现 职责： 引擎程序 工具： C++、Prime Engine

做了什么 这个项目是在 Prime Engine 上做修改和扩展：这是一个用于玩法与渲染实验的精简 C++ 游戏引擎。 我实现了多个引擎级系统，包括： 用于渲染优化的视锥剔除 层级式 BVH 剔除（静态中位数划分树与动态 Morton 编码树） 碰撞检测与物理运动（带重力的移动-滑动） 骨骼动画混合（全身、局部与叠加层） 包围体与动画状态的调试可视化工具 要在引擎内部动手，得先摸清它的组件系统、事件系统和场景图结构，才能在不破坏原有架构的前提下加入新功能。 主要成果 读懂并上手了一个大型 C++ 代码库：一个精简的自研游戏引擎。 实现了视锥剔除，用于渲染优化。 构建了层级式 BVH 剔除，包含静态中位数划分树与动态 Morton 编码树。 实现了基础物理系统，具备碰撞与滑动响应。 构建了骨骼动画混合系统，支持全身、局部与叠加混合。 亮点 Frustum culling BVH culling Morton codes Physics engine Collision & sliding Animation blending Additive layers Debug visualization C++ 如何实现 视锥剔除 为减少不必要的渲染开销，我使用包围盒实现了视锥剔除。 步骤： 计算每个网格实例的世界空间包围盒。 使用投影-视图矩阵将包围盒顶点变换到裁剪空间。 将包围盒与相机视锥的六个平面进行测试。 若所有顶点都位于某一平面之外，则该物体被标记为剔除并在渲染时跳过。 我还为包围盒添加了调试渲染，以直观验证剔除逻辑的正确性。当大量物体位于相机视野之外时，这显著减少了送入渲染器的物体数量。 视锥剔除流程 Mesh Instance world bounding box Transform corners to clip space Frustum Test 6 plane

的物体数量。 视锥剔除流程 Mesh Instance world bounding box Transform corners to clip space Frustum Test 6 plane checks Cull Render outside any plane intersects / inside Bounding box corners are transformed and tested against the camera frustum to skip off-screen objects. 物理与碰撞系统 我实现了一个简单的碰撞体系统，支持附加在游戏对象上的球体与盒体碰撞器。 每一帧： 碰撞体根据所属对象的变换更新其世界空间位置。 当对象尝试移动时，引擎会在候选位置检测碰撞。 若发生碰撞，则将移动向量投影到碰撞平面上，去除指向表面的分量。 剩余的移动分量使对象沿障碍物自然滑动。 重力 对于受重力影响的对象： 每帧根据重力加速度更新竖直方向速度。 将竖直方向的移动分量施加到对象上。 若检测到地面支撑，则重置竖直速度，以防止持续向下加速。 这既产生自然的下落效果，又能防止物体穿透地面。 物理：带重力的移动-滑动 Input Move desired displacement Apply Gravity update vertical velocity Collision Query check candidate position No Hit → Move Hit Surface remove normal component Slide move along tangent gravity Candidate movement is tested for collisions; if blocked, the engine cancels motio

ng tangent gravity Candidate movement is tested for collisions; if blocked, the engine cancels motion into the surface and keeps the tangential part for sliding. 动画混合 我为士兵的动画状态机扩展了多种混合模式，在生成时通过来自 Lua 的 npcType 值选择。骨架被划分为上半身（关节 2–37）与下半身（关节 38–47），因此不同片段可同时驱动不同部位。 全身混合：按权重混合两个片段（例如待机 + 奔跑，各占 0.5）。 局部混合：某个片段通过 PARTIAL_BODY_ANIMATION 仅覆盖某一关节范围（例如在全身待机之上叠加上半身射击）。 叠加混合：将某个片段 叠加 在现有姿势之上，而非替换它。 为实现叠加运动，我增加了一个 ADDITIVE 槽位标志。叠加阶段将片段相对其参考姿势（第 0 帧）的增量作为仅含旋转的四元数，并左乘到关节的局部变换上，这样基础动作保持不变，而额外动作被叠加其上（例如下半身奔跑 + 上半身射击，边跑边瞄准）。 动画混合模式 Full-body blend STAND + RUN 0.5 / 0.5 across all joints Partial blend Shoot (2–37) STAND (lower) upper-body override replaces joints 2–37 Additive blend STAND + Shoot RUN (38–47) layered on top delta pose added to upper body 层级式剔除：静态与动态 BVH 我将视锥剔除扩展为由中央 CullingManager 管理的包围体层级（B

layered on top delta pose added to upper body 层级式剔除：静态与动态 BVH 我将视锥剔除扩展为由中央 CullingManager 管理的包围体层级（BVH），从而只需少量 AABB 与视锥的测试即可剔除大批物体，而非对每个网格逐一测试。每个实例被归类为静态或动态。 静态 BVH：一次性构建，沿质心分布最广的轴递归进行中位数划分，叶节点最多容纳 8 个实例。遍历时会剪除包围盒未命中视锥的整棵子树。 动态 BVH：针对运动物体每帧重建（为了给它加压测试，我生成了大量随机运动的小网格）。质心被归一化并编码为 30 位 Morton 码（Z 序），排序后在中点划分：一种线性、对缓存友好的构建方式。 静态树对建筑与地形能提供更高的质量，而 Morton 码重建足够廉价，可对大量运动单位每帧执行，Z 序使空间上相近的物体在内存中也相邻，因此构建与遍历几乎线性地访问节点，保持缓存友好。两者的复杂度都约为 O(n log n) ，主要由排序决定。 静态与动态 BVH 的构建对比 Static BVH — median split root AABB split on widest axis · leaves ≤ 8 instances built once for static geometry Dynamic BVH — Morton (Z-order) Morton code → sort → midpoint split rebuilt every frame for moving objects 静态 BVH：从根节点（红色）到叶节点（紫色）的彩虹色包围盒。 动态 BVH：覆盖在随机运动网格上的白色包围盒，叶节点以圆点表示。 ← 返回项目

词云 二维码生成器 词云生成器 在下方粘贴文本，然后点击“生成”。 Text input Nothing Can Go Wrong is a design exploration in indirect control and consequence-driven planning. Instead of piloting the protagonist moment-to-moment, players design a route and then watch their plan unfold, for better or worse, as environmental objects react in surprising ways. The appeal—and the difficulty—comes from predicting emergent interactions while still feeling empowered by a plan that was yours. My responsibilities covered concept ideation, systems design, engineering, and integration of artist-created assets in Godot. The core loop began with a lightweight pathing tool, but the heart of the experience emerged from environmental 'verbs': train tracks, whirlpools, spring pads, trapdoors, and chain reactions that could delight or derai

': train tracks, whirlpools, spring pads, trapdoors, and chain reactions that could delight or derail. Balancing readability with surprise required strict rules and effective feedback animation that help the player learn the world's grammar. Difficulty tuning was driven by data rather than guesswork; I exposed parameters for moving speeds, impulse strength, and trigger sequencing, allowing tight loops of test–tweak–test within the jam's limited timeframe. The art pass focused on clarity and charm: silhouettes that read at a glance, restrained palettes, and motion that communicates function. We achieved a polished, playable demo on schedule and ranked in the top 20% overall. Post-jam, I'd extend the system with better text and visual explanation so players can gradually learn the mechanics,

end the system with better text and visual explanation so players can gradually learn the mechanics, add optional objectives for mastery, and implement robust replays to encourage sharing. Most importantly, the project validated a personal design interest: agency through foresight. When players laugh at a plan that almost worked—and instantly want to iterate—you've got a loop worth growing. 最大词数： 旋转： 0° 0°/90° 缩放： √n n log n 生成 下载 SVG 下载 PNG 参考： Jason Davies’ Word Cloud 二维码生成器 在下方输入文本或网址，然后点击“生成”。 QR code text input 尺寸 (px)： 生成 下载 PNG 二维码预览将显示在此处。 参考： QRCode.js
介绍一下王元辰（Yuanchen Wang）：他是一名游戏开发者，正在南加州大学攻读计算机科学硕士（游戏开发方向），做过战斗设计、玩法编程和游戏引擎开发。

chunks.section_title: 介绍一下王元辰这个人
chunks.text: 介绍一下王元辰这个人：王元辰是一名游戏开发者，做游戏设计、玩法编程和引擎开发，也做过科研。
王元辰个人简介：一名游戏开发者，正在读游戏开发方向的计算机硕士，擅长战斗设计、玩法编程与引擎开发。
关于王元辰这个人：他是做游戏的开发者，擅长战斗设计、玩法编程和引擎开发，之前还做过科研。
王元辰的简历亮点：游戏战斗设计、玩法编程、关卡设计，以及 C++ 游戏引擎开发经验。
王元辰的教育背景：南加州大学计算机科学硕士（游戏开发）、哈佛医学院生物医学信息学硕士、罗切斯特大学脑与认知科学学士与心理学学士。
王元辰在南加州大学读游戏开发方向的计算机硕士，本科在罗切斯特大学读脑与认知科学与心理学。
王元辰的学历：计算机科学硕士（游戏开发方向）、生物医学信息学硕士，以及脑与认知科学学士和心理学学士。
王元辰在 Cemented Dreams 里做战斗设计，设计并实现了钩爪位移与滑行战斗等核心战斗移动机制。
王元辰负责玩法编程与关卡设计，用 Unreal Engine 的 C++ 与 Blueprint 构建移动与战斗系统。
王元辰在 Prime Engine 做 C++ 引擎开发，实现了视锥剔除、BVH 剔除、碰撞与移动滑行物理、骨骼动画混合。
王元辰会用 Unreal Engine 5、Unity 和 Godot 这三款游戏引擎做游戏开发。
王元辰独自开发了解谜游戏 Gyrotris，用 Godot 完成设计、编程与像素美术，是他第一款完整发布的作品。
王元辰在 game jam 中担任主程序，做了 Nothing Can Go Wrong 与 Codebreaker 的核心玩法系统。

chunks.section_title: 实时渲染与着色器项目
chunks.text: 王元辰在 Unity URP 里做实时渲染，用 HLSL 写程序化熔岩与裂缝着色器，实现小行星撞击特效。
王元辰用 Onshape CAD 参数化建模，复刻了 Xenoblade 2 的 Aegis Sword 道具。
王元辰发表过神经科学与医学影像方向的论文，涉及 fMRI、催产素、语义分割等主题。
王元辰为本作品集网站做了一个 AI 智能问答助手，用检索增强生成技术回答访客关于他项目与经历的问题。
